title: Five-eighth

infobox.value: or **stand-off** is one of the [positions](/wiki/Rugby_league_positions "Rugby league positions") in a [rugby league](/wiki/Rugby_league "Rugby league") football team. Wearing jersey number 6, this player is one of the two [half-backs](/wiki/Rugby_league_positions#Scrum-half_/_half-back "Rugby league positions") in a team, partnering the [scrum-half](/wiki/Rugby_league_positions#Scrum-half "Rugby league positions").[[1]](#cite_note-1)[[2]](#cite_note-2)[[3]](#cite_note-3) As the name suggests, the five-eighth is the go between the half-back and the [threequarters](/wiki/Rugby_league_positions#Threequarters "Rugby league positions") (5⁄8 is between 1⁄2 and 3⁄4), moving the ball from the contested middle to the outside backs.[[4]](#cite_note-4) In a traditional 'back-line' attack (No. 1-7),[[5]](#cite_note-5) the five-eighth, sometimes known as the pivot or second receiver,[[6]](#cite_note-6) receives the ball from the half-back,[[7]](#cite_note-7) who is the first receiver of the ball from the dummy-half or [hooker](/wiki/Hooker_(rugby_league) "Hooker (rugby league)") following a [tackle](/wiki/Tackle_(football_move) "Tackle (football move)").

["The NRL's 10 best halves combos"](http://www.nrl.com/the-nrls-10-best-halves-combos/tabid/10874/newsid/70978/default.aspx). *NRL.com*. [National Rugby League](/wiki/National_Rugby_League "National Rugby League"). 6 March 2013. Retrieved 4 September 2013.

Archived from [the original](http://wwos.ninemsn.com.au/article.aspx?id=1051684) on 16 October 2014. Retrieved 4 September 2013.

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Half back position in rugby league

This article is about the rugby league position. For the rugby union positions, see [First five-eighths](/wiki/First_five-eighths "First five-eighths") and [Second five-eighths](/wiki/Second_five-eighths "Second five-eighths"). For other uses, see [5/8](/wiki/5/8_(disambiguation) "5/8 (disambiguation)").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f6/Thomas_Bosc.jpg/250px-Thomas_Bosc.jpg)](/wiki/File:Thomas_Bosc.jpg)

Stand-offs such as France's [Thomas Bosc](/wiki/Thomas_Bosc "Thomas Bosc") require good passing skills.

**Five-eighth** or **stand-off** is one of the [positions](/wiki/Rugby_league_positions "Rugby league positions") in a [rugby league](/wiki/Rugby_league "Rugby league") football team. Wearing jersey number 6, this player is one of the two [half-backs](/wiki/Rugby_league_positions#Scrum-half_/_half-back "Rugby league positions") in a team, partnering the [scrum-half](/wiki/Rugby_league_positions#Scrum-half "Rugby league positions").[[1]](#cite_note-1)[[2]](#cite_note-2)[[3]](#cite_note-3) As the name suggests, the five-eighth is the go between the half-back and the [threequarters](/wiki/Rugby_league_positions#Threequarters "Rugby league positions") (5⁄8 is between 1⁄2 and 3⁄4), moving the ball from the contested middle to the outside backs.[[4]](#cite_note-4) In a traditional 'back-line' attack (No. 1-7),[[5]](#cite_note-5) the five-eighth, sometimes known as the pivot or second receiver,[[6]](#cite_note-6) receives the ball from the half-back,[[7]](#cite_note-7) who is the first receiver of the ball from the dummy-half or [hooker](/wiki/Hooker_(rugby_league) "Hooker (rugby league)") following a [tackle](/wiki/Tackle_(football_move) "Tackle (football move)").

The role of the five-eighth is often to pass the ball away from the congested area around the tackle, further out along the 'back-line' to the outside backs (the centres and wingers) who have more space to run with.[[8]](#cite_note-8)[[9]](#cite_note-9) Furthermore, players in this position typically assume responsibility for kicking the ball for field position in general play.[[10]](#cite_note-10)[[11]](#cite_note-11) The five-eighth is therefore considered one of the most important positions, often referred to as a 'play maker', assuming a decision-making role on the field.[[12]](#cite_note-12)[[13]](#cite_note-13) Over time, however, as the game has evolved, the roles of the two halves have grown more aligned and difficult to distinguish.[[14]](#cite_note-14) Along with other key positions – [fullback](/wiki/Fullback_(rugby_league) "Fullback (rugby league)"), hooker and half-back – the five-eighth makes up what is known as a team's spine.[[15]](#cite_note-15)

One book published in 1996 stated that in senior rugby league, the five-eighth and hooker handled the ball more often than any other positions.[[16]](#cite_note-16)

The [Rugby League International Federation](/wiki/Rugby_League_International_Federation "Rugby League International Federation")'s [Laws of the Game](/wiki/Laws_of_rugby_league "Laws of rugby league") state that the "Stand-off half or Five-eighth" is to be numbered 6.[[17]](#cite_note-17) However, traditionally players' jersey numbers have varied, and in the modern [Super League](/wiki/Super_League "Super League"), each squad's players are assigned individual numbers regardless of position.

## Etymology

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Five-eighth&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Etymology")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/bc/Wally_Lewis_%2829_April_2004%2C_Brisbane%29.jpg/250px-Wally_Lewis_%2829_April_2004%2C_Brisbane%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Wally_Lewis_(29_April_2004,_Brisbane).jpg)

[Wally Lewis](/wiki/Wally_Lewis "Wally Lewis") was voted Australia's greatest ever five-eighth in 2008.

Traditionally in [rugby football](/wiki/Rugby_football "Rugby football"), there have always been two half-backs as well as [scrums](/wiki/Scrum_(rugby) "Scrum (rugby)") involving the forwards. Of the two half-backs, the name "scrum half" was given to the one who was involved in the scrum by feeding the ball into it; and the name "stand-off half" was given to the one who stood off to the side of the scrum.[[18]](#cite_note-18) In Britain, where rugby league originated, this terminology has been retained.[[19]](#cite_note-19) In [Australian English](/wiki/Australian_English "Australian English"), however, "five-eighth" is the term used for the number 6, to differentiate from the "half-back" which is the name commonly given to the number 7.[[20]](#cite_note-20) In New Zealand, both terms appear to be used interchangeably.

## Notable Five-eighths

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Five-eighth&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Notable Five-eighths")]

Five-eighths that feature in their respective nations' rugby league halls of fame are England's [Roger Millward](/wiki/Roger_Millward "Roger Millward"), Australia's [Wally Lewis](/wiki/Wally_Lewis "Wally Lewis"), [Bob Fulton](/wiki/Bob_Fulton "Bob Fulton"), [Brett Kenny](/wiki/Brett_Kenny "Brett Kenny"), [Albert Rosenfeld](/wiki/Albert_Rosenfeld "Albert Rosenfeld") and [Vic Hey](/wiki/Vic_Hey "Vic Hey"), and New Zealand's [George Menzies](/wiki/George_Menzies "George Menzies").

Rugby league's first known black player, [Lucius Banks](/wiki/Lucius_Banks "Lucius Banks"), played in the position for [Hunslet R.L.F.C.](/wiki/Hunslet_R.L.F.C. "Hunslet R.L.F.C.") in 1912-13.[[21]](#cite_note-21)

## See also

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Five-eighth&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: See also")]

* [Rugby league positions](/wiki/Rugby_league_positions "Rugby league positions")
* [Rugby league gameplay](/wiki/Rugby_league_gameplay "Rugby league gameplay")
